md:
At some indeterminate point in my childhood, my deathly fear of ghosts and their ilk transitioned into a vague fetishization, one that was surprisingly logistically justifiable.

Relatedly, who has ever even feared a male ghost? What is there to fear from the ghost of a male (be it an adolescent or an adult)? Nothing. A confrontation with a male ghost (even if it were a hopeless affair for the living) excites in one the naive, bestial instinct to engage in a duel, one of laughably mismatched equals perhaps, but equals nonetheless. The same can obviously not be said for female ghosts. Why is this so? One could draw some crude gendered parallels and appeal to a notion of a *truly dangerous* female form being oxymoronic, and while one would be correct in identifying femininity as crucial to this unique fear inspired by these entities, there's surely something more to it... something that goes beyond human feminine nature in a way that isn't emulated by their male equivalents.
